. CURRENT AFFAIRB,

We learn from the Return issued by the Department,

of the Banking and Railway Statistics that the total
receipts from passengers on the various railways of Ire-
land during 1903 amounted to £1,669,831 the total re-
ceipts from goods trafiic amounted to £1,845,003. It
will thus be scen that in 1903 the value of goods traffic
was about £200,000 greater than the value gf passen-
ger traffic. How does this fact strike one in view of the
~ efforts made on the one hand by the railways to increase
passenger traffic and the lack of effort to stimulate
* goods trafic! Our economic sense was shocked some
ears back when the then Chairman of the poor old
 Midland Railway referred to the est;nbhshmqnb of one
or two extra race courses near the line—as if Ireland
as not far more of these non-productive gambling play-
‘gr'ounds' than her slender economic shoulders can bear—
‘welcoma source of new traffic to the company. The

ways have puﬁ out a great deal of energy in encourag-

ing tourist and all kinds of passenger traffic. Excur-
sions here, there and everywhere, follow one another
almost daily. From a railway point of view, we take no
exception to that display of energy ; but what we would
like to point out is the contrast between the energy
shown in increasing passenger traffic, and the apathy,
or worse, displayed in dealing with goods ; .and yeb
goods  traffic, notwithstanding the way in which it is
hampered, brings in about £200,000 in a year mor¢
than passengers, notwithstanding the way in which,
compamtivcly, they are pampcrcd.

We have a recollection that someone suggested, we
think it was in our own columns, that the railways
should initiate a system of goods traffic excursions as
well as passenger excursions. Why not the railway
termini have announcements such as “goods taken
from such a place to such a place on such a date at half
the usual rates.”” Whether that is practicable or not,
it is plain that in a country, the railways of which take
about £200,000 in a year more for carrying goods than
they do for carrying passengers, there is ample room:
for the exercise of ability in stimulating goods traffic.
Such a stimulation would be good for the receipts of the
railways, and it would also be good for the economic
interests of the country.

We learn from the same Return that on the 3lst
December, 1903, there were no less than 500,226 sepa-
rate accounts open in the Post Office and Trustees’ Sav-
ings Banks in Ireland. That means that more than one
person in every nine in Ireland, including the babies,
have an account in a savings bank. In 1884, though
the population was considerably larger, there were only
177,628 accounts open.

The mid-summer number of the Newbridge College
Magazine, as usual, is the first out of the three College
summer publications. ~We are anxiously waiting for
the appearance of the Castleknock Chronicle an the
Clongownian. The magazine of the popular and rising
Trish Ireland College of the Dominican Fathers at
Newbridge is, as usual, very interesting. One of the
well-known and accomplished literary men of the Domi-
nican Order in Ireland, Father Ambrose Coleman, con-
tributes an article (illustrated) on “ Dominican Colleges
in the Philippines.”” “ A Figure of the Past ’ is an in-
teresting illustrated article on O’Carolan. The Very
Rev. Father Lyons, Provincial, visited the College dur-
ing last October. He lectured there, and, in the course
of his lecture, he said :— Many of you, being so young,
do not realise the necessity of availing yourselves of the
opportunities afforded you at present of becoming fervent
Catholics and good Irishmen; you have an able staff of
Professors, and I find the Fathers are doing everything
they can for your comfort and happiness. I am glad to
hear you are all in thorough sympathy with the TIrish
Language Movement.” Much has been done in the Col-
lege for the revival of our National Language, but much
still remains to be done. I have no doubt but you:will
all foster the Irish spirit that pervades the entire institu-
tion; and that you are justly proud of the noble title

‘by which your dima Mater is known outside its walls, as”

The Trish Ireland College.”’”’ These remarks indicate
the spirit that pervades the College. We believe it is
often said, by way of excuse on bebalf of other Colleges
that are not known outside their walls as Irish Ireland
Colleges, that the parents are to blame. It is passing
strango that the parents of the very large roll of stu-
dents in Newbridge College do not send in a round-robin
to the patriotic prior, Father O'Neill, demanding a West
British atmosphere, or else that their boys will be taken

from Newbridge and sent to a Ping-Pong College! Can
it be that, asin some “ all-creeds-all-classes '’ circles, one
“saved ’’ can wag nineteen * Idolators’’ in the name of
“Tolerance,” in certain Colleges other than New-
bridge, in Leinster, and St. Colman’s, Fermoy, in' Mun-
ster, two or three peoinin parents-are able to call
the tune for the whole institution?

The annual meeting of the Tyrone County Council
was held recently. Amongst those present was onc W.
Coote, J.P. Two men were co-opted members, and we
read in the report of the meeting in the Belfast News
Lctter that “ The Nationalist members proposed’ that
Mr. Michael Lynch or Mr. John Corr be co-opted, but
the proposition was defeated.”” One’ Mi: Gunning-
Moore proposed the re-election of the Duke of Aber-
corn, who occupies such an exalted position as that of
Grand Master of the Freemasons of Ireland, as Chair-
man of the County Council. And who do our readers
think seconded this proposition. W. Coote, J.P.? Not
at all. Tt was seconded by one George Murnaghan,
Nationalist M.P.! This M.P. said that- Abercorn was
entitled to the position! The Duke, in returning
thanks, said—“ He had also to thank Mr. Murnaghan
for the far too complimentary words in which he had
seconded the resolution.”’” Even the Duke thought' that
the Nationalist M.P. was “far too complimentary.”
Probably this stalwart member of the Irish Parliamen-
tary Party was delighted at being spoken of by a real
live Unionist Duke, who is likewise Grand Master of
the Free and Accepted Masons of Ireland.

The Irish Hierarchy have founded a Scholarship Fund
to provide scholarships tenable at University College,
Dublin for boys, and at the Dominican College,
Eccles Street, or Loreto College, Stephen’s Green for
girls. The Bishops have started the fund with a sum
of £1,000, and it is hoped that private benefactors will
follow suit ; there is now a chance for the wealthy Irish
Americans to give an earnest of their regard for their
motherland. The President and Professors of Univer-
sity College announce, through Rev. Dr. Delany, S.J.,
a contribution of £500, and we note that Rev. Mon-
signor Molloy, though decidedly somewhat of a “ tame’
Catholic, announces that he will subscribe £100 on the
1st of November, if by that nine others will have sub-
scribed £100 each. No doubt, also, Blackrock and
Castleknock will not be slow to follow the generous
example set by Clongowes in the matter of special
college scholarships.

The Department has done something, be it much or
little, for the growing of tobacco in Ireland What has
it done for the legitimate protection of the manufac-
turers of “ Irish Roll?’ “Irish Roll,”’ as Messrs. Gal-
lagher, the well-known tobacco manufacturers, point
out, is not a trade mark, and consequently there is no
“:gal disability preventing similar tobacco being manu-
factured in England and Scotland, and being sent over
here as the genuine article.  “Irish Roll”’ made in
England is a rather unfair competitor for Irish manu-
facturers to have to compete against. But, in addition
to that, this British “Irish Roll,”’ as has frequently
been pointed out in these columns, is being sold below
cost price with a view to killing out the Irish-manufac-

_ tured Irish Roll. Messrs. Gallagher point out that
though British manufacturers are selling this stuff
under cost, yet the Irish consumer pays the same for
his ounce of the bogus Irish Roll. made in Britain as he
does for the genuine article made at home. It is evi-
.dently to the interest of the Irish consumer that he
should be at pains to see that when buying Irish Roll
he receives the genuine article. By allowing the
suprious Irish Roll to be palmed off on him, he receives
even: no immediate benefit, although he helps in the
palpable attempt to kill an industry which, if killed,
would land the consumer into the tender hands of the
British manufacturers.

The Belfast News Letter, commenting on the outrage
on the statue of King William III. at Boyle, says:—
“ A nice sort of place Boyle must be for Protestants and
Loyalists to live in.”’ ‘We are not aware f.!m.b it was
Nationalists who beheaded the statue of William III.
Bo that as it may, what sort of a place does the Belfast
News Letter think Carnthall, Co. Tyrone, 'where (':he
man Coote, Justice of the Peace, comes _from, is for Irish
Catholic sewing instructresses to live in?

Maynooth was an interesting place on Tljursday last.
In thz course of the brief speech with which the Car-
dinal opened the proceedings of the Ma'ynoobh Union,
he said :—*TI do not believe, certainly in my memory,
that the Government of the country was ever in such
an unsatisfactory state, as far as Catholic interests are
concerned, as at the present moment _(applause)‘. The
country is being governed not only without t:akmg into
consideration Catholic views or the views or interests of
the Catholic majority, it is being governed in open and
candid opposition to them.”” And again he remarked—
“In the past we had a Chief Secretary to the Lord
Licutenant of Ireland, but we have now a Chief Secre-
tary to the Orangemen of Ireland (loud applause), and
it is not the fault of the Orangemen if they have not
got an Under-Secretary also (applause) ; they are \vorlf-
ing hard for it (applause). Well, the result of that is
that in dealing with the government of the country the
majority, the Catholic majority, are not only thrown
aside, but flouted, not ouly by officials, but also} by
some of the Boards of the country, who take our views
and wishes very little into consideration when devising
schemes either for the education of the country or any
other purpose.”

The Most Rev. Dr. O’Donnell, in the course of his
paper on the University, or rather lack-University,
question, well said :—* In public affairs it is not enough
to deserve success. As the world goes, organised deter-
mination carries further than the merits of a cause.”
Quite so; if the Catholics do not take off their coats
and fight for Tolerance, Justice, and Fair Play, they
may go whistle for a redress of their many and cruel
grievances.  His lordship remarked:—“By British
policy we were despoiled, and Trinity College has the
spoil.””  Another remark of his lordship worth quot-
ing was:—“ There is a claim anterior to the cry ¢ Hands
off Trinity College.” It is ‘ Hands off the revenues of
Irish monasteries,” which have passed to no private
owner.’’ <

There are now three sets of views concerning the Uni-
versity question. The Bishop of Raphoe looks to agita-
tion for redress; there is Father Finlay’s ad interim
plan, and thirdly, there is Rev. Dr. M‘Donald’s sugges-
tion that Catholics should go into “ Trinity ”’ as a con-
scious body of Catholic Nationalists.

Dr. M‘Donald vigorously advocated going boldly in-
side the gates of “ Trinity ” and fighting the battle for
cnlightenment, faith, and fatherland on the enemy’s
ground. e said:—*“ Could we not take courage and
carry the war into the camp—the very citadel—of the
onomy? If the Saxon in Ireland is to be fought and
beaten, why not fight him and beat him in Trinity it-
self? To this plan two objections have been taken,
based on two dangers—to Faith and to Nationality.
Ho (the. speaker) recognised these dangers; but were
there not greater? Was there not a danger that with
the economic decline of our people, their transfer by
emigration to the anti-Catholic atmosphere of other
countries, the lack of love of prayer, religious education

for our laymen, which, withoub faculties of theology

. Dr. M'Donald has evidently thought out the situa-

For our part, we do not think so in the

when some particluar line of policy has plainly the 
majority of opinion behind to cry out for unity in action. 
In the meantime, however sceptical people may be of

do no harm by making themselves as troublesome as

Else-
where is this issue we publish two individual views on

by om· readers. 
Rev. Dr. O'Riordan's paper on" Tolerance and Tole-
ration ,r was just such a masterly piece of work as our 
readers who know his powers would have expected from 
him . 
Here is an extract from it :-" I take up Thom's 
Almanac of half a century ago, and I find tha.t the pub-
lic offices were occupied almost exclusively by non-Ca-
tholics, from the Lord Lieutenancy clown to the Clerk-
ship of Petty Sessions( hear, hea.r). 
A generation be-
fore that time it was so clown to the office of the local 
process-server (hear, hear). 
Now, how, did it come to 
pass that Catholics were left outside and that non-Ca-
tholics were left within 1 Not surely that Catholics wil-
lingly yielded all public positions to their neighbours. 
The arrangement was, therefore, made by the other 
side. 
And what was the reason of ·the monopoly 1 Not, 
surely that all Catholics were incapable of any civil 
function un·less that of paying rates. 
The conclusion i,J 
clea,r; that exclusion was the s0cial penalty of Ca.tho 
Jicism, and that monopoly was the social reward of the 
other side (applause). 
Now that appears to me to be 
religious intolerance with the penalty of distributive in-
justice. 
The Catholics have been gradually workin g-
their way towards civil equality; but every step lms 
been disputed, and every claim has been • met with the 
cry of intolerance, and with a protest against intruding 
religion where it should have no place; which, being 
interpreted, means that they secured foi· themselves by 
religious 
exclusiveness privileges of which 
Catholics 
claim their share by civil right. 
Catholics then had 
either to remain resigned to the condition of slaves with-
out rights, or to protest against their faith being mad~ 
a bar between them and civil justice. In doing -so, they 
hav~ not sought to intrude religion into pure_ly ci~il 
a1ffa1rs, hut they have sought to extrude religious m-
tolerance which had taken up its abode there without 
saying_ '_by your leave.' If our neighbours are privileged 
by _reh~~us _exclusiveness they call it civil right, if we 
chum civil right they call it ·religious exch1siveness." 
The discussion on Dr. ◊'Riordan's paper, as briefly 
reported in the Press, was very interesting and signifi-
cant. 
Father ·Philip O'Doherty, of Derry, said:-
" They 'in the North of Ireland ·had felt very strongly 
about the 'Catholic Association. 
Of necessity · their 
hearts went with it. 'Their _population was dying out, 
and their country was <;lying out, and the chances were 
that 'Ireland mig'ht yet '.be a Pretestant .country through 
an organisation on one side a:ncl. the want of it qn the 
.other. 
The Irish ·catholics had been tolerating them-
selves out of existence." 
The .Most Rev .. Dr. O'Dea, 13ishop of Clonfert, said-
" He was in favour of a policy of broadminded tolera-
tfon. 
But he did not think they should be tolerant of 
the intolerance that was practised against them (hear, 
hear). 
The facts brought out from time to time at the 
Maynooth Union ·proved conclusively that they, the 
Catholic Celts, ·-were stra.ngel"S in their own land. The 
public offices were -almost ·exclusively monopolised by 
the Ascendancy party. 
He believed action was needed. 
The supreme int0lcrance • to 'Which they were subjected 
in this country was an intolerance which excluded the 
best intellects of the country from developing the gifts 
which Goel had given them. 
He confessed that 
from 
the 
beginning 
his 
own 
sympathies 
were 
co.rdially 
with 
the 
Catholic 
Association." 
His 
lordship 
also .stated that:-" It was the opinion 
of many who held prominent positions in Ireland 
that if an association were established for the defence of 
C'atholic interests, and adopted a reasonable line of ac-
tion, that that association should have the sympathy 
and support of all honest-minded Irish Catholics." 
For om· part, in season and out of season, we have 
kept before our readers the vital need of an organised 
.fight for Tolerance, Justice, and Fair Play. 
When over 
a year ago the anti-Irish bigots began to howl and 
slander, and a sort of panic seized the hearts of the 
timid Papists, we, at least, kept the flag flying in face 
of all the enemies from all the points of the compass. 
And many of our anti-Irish bigot enemies did their 
little best to hit us, but we only laughed at them; it 
was our business to stand to om· guns, and we stood. 
Though many of the bigots did their little worst against 
us, it must be put clown to their credit that no "unau-
thorised" bigots ever held us up. 
The University 
debacle and other recent developments ought, by this 
time, have opened, or re-opened, many eyes to the neces-
sity of an organised stand. 
Neither the Inde11e·ndent 
nor the Freem a.n referred editorially to the Bishop .of 
Clonfert's remarks. Evidently the voice of one bishop 
is not charged with sufficient compelling current to send 
the editors of these two papers scampe1·ing helter-skelter 
up the Pastoral Kopje. 
A correspondent makes a suggestion with reference 
to the election of an Executive of the Gaelic Leaaue. 
His view is that besides the branches and the· C'o1~•'0 c 
Ce"1111c ... ,q, there should be County Councils and Pro-
vincial Oouncils, and that the representatives of the 
various provincial Councils should form the Governino 
Body. It appears to us that any step in the directio~ 
of creating separate constituencies, each of which would 
elect one or more members on the Governing Body, 
members who would be immediately responsible to the 
constituency that elected them, would be an advance iu 
a progressive direction. If we had an Irish Parlia.meut 
a system by which delegates coming from various part~ 
of the country elected members in the lump, members 
immediately responsible to no particular constituencies 
would scarcely be considered a good workina arrange: 
ment. 
We think the .suggestion of our cor;espondent 
is wort11y of the careful consideration of all anxious to 
strengthen the foundations upon wT1ich the. organisation 
rests. 
We ~re sorry t? hea~· that there is no prospect of the

lege, Waterford, coming off this year.

Bto’ther Berchin’s fault however. Last St. Patrick’s
night Tavs Saop by Father Peter O’Leary was pro-
duced by the students, and was well received by the
Irish Ireland majority. An attempt was recently made
to introduce a kind of football known by the name of
soccer, whatever that is, and for a time it was the game
in the College; but owing to the continued agitation of
the Irish section, the game was forced outside the walls,
On the 7th of June, the feast of St. John Baptiste de
la Salle, an operotta entitled “ Northman and Saxon ’’
was performed. We learn that it was accorded an in-
different reception and that the Irish section of the
students audibly expressed their disapproval of the
piece.  The operetta concerned Alfred, the English
King, and the Danes.

Messrs. M. H. Gill and Son, Ltd., have secured the
copyright, etc., of the well known series of religious
publications entitled “ The Little Treasury of Leaflets.””
We understand the competition was very keen, and in-
cluded some foreign firms. We congratulate Messrs.
Gill and Son on their enterprise in keeping the publica-
tions at home.

Ireland is not alone in Moore celebrations. By a
Chicago paper sent us we see that the Irish Historical
Society in that city, to commemorate the poet’s birth-
day, recently held a very successful entertainment, in-
cluding concert and dance, in which his music was ren-

dered.

"We received some samples of Irish-manufactured boot
polish styled Valine. The name of the firm who sent
them was a good guarantee of merit, but as they were
sent to us for a trial we distributed them for that pur-
pose. The report is of those whom we asked to test them
that they are excellent. Valine Polish is of three
colours, black, white and tan, and is made up in three-
penny and sixpenny bottles or tubes.

The Catholic Association is still a dreadful night-
mare to some people. In the Report of the Home Mis-
sion presented to the General Assembly of the Presbyte-
rians, we read :— In our report of last year we referred
to the so-called ‘ Roman Catholic Association;’ during
the year we have not heard much of this particular
organisation. We have, however, only too much reason
to believe that the spirit which animated it has been at
work, and with results in the direction desired and
aimed at—results that make against the best interests
of our country.”” The members who attended the
General Assembly must have had a sleepless night after
reading the remarks of the Bishop of Clonfert at the
Maynooth Union.

Some speakers at the General Assembly of the Pres-
byterians do not like the Irish language. During the
discussion on the Report presented by the Committee of
Elementary Education, Rev. J. D. Osborne, who se-
conded the adoption of the Report, said that “he did
not see why the teaching of Irish should be paid for at
the double rate of any other language.” ~ Rev. Dr.
Workman “ protested against 10s. being paid for the
‘teaching of Irish while only 5s. was paid for the teach-
ing of French and Latin.” It might be “sectarian’’
and “bigoted ”’ if we suggested that the Rev. Dr.
‘Workman, Presbyterian Minister, is not a sound Irish-
man! Rev. Dr. Wilson said “he was opposed to the

- teaching of Irish, and he could inform them that the

Treasury, after July, 1906, would pay nothing whatever
for that subject.”

In the course of some ‘‘ Assembly Notes’’ in the
ic Belfast News Letter, written “ By a Member,”
—“ Referring to the teaching of Irish in na-

THE LEADER.

tional schools, Mr.. Osborne condemned strongly the
high fees paid for it, and cited an instance in which &
teachor was paid £125 in addition to his income for
teaching Irish. The Assembly breathed more freely
when Dr. Wilson, himself a Commissioner of Education,
informed them that he and Dr. Taylox rggm‘ded the
fees which were paid for the teaching of Irish as a dis-
tinct fraud, and also that after July, 1906, no more
fees for Irish would be given by the Government.’ A
mere Irish Catholic Nationalist might run the risk of
being “held up "’ by the non-sectarian Irish Republi-
cans as a foul and nefarious traitor if he let himself go
too freely in this matter. Are not Dr. Wilson, Dr.
Taylor and the members of the Presbyterian General
Assembly who “breathed miore freely,” fellow-“Irish-
men,”” and is not the man a base traitor who denies that
Ireland cannot afford to lose a single one of her sons.
We being “bigots,”” “ traitors’’ and stirrers up of
“strife ’’ are strongly of opinion that Ireland not only
could spare, but would be decidedly the gainer, if a
goodly bundle of the anti-Irish monstrosities that are
supposed to be our fellow-countrymen took single tickets
to Hong Kong or some such place.

At a meeting of General Assembly of the Presby-
terian Church in Ireland, the Rev. J. B. Armour said :
“ They protested about Mr. Balfour asserting that the
Belfast Queen’s College was a Presbyterian institution,
and went on to say that it was undenominational in
character, and so on. Well he (the speaker) thought
that the President of that college was a Presbyterian ;
he thought also that the professors of that college were
either Presbyterians or belonged to Protestant denomi-
nations. He was not aware that there was a single
Roman Catholic professor in the Queen’s College, Bel-
fast. Its atmosphere was the pure ozone of Ulster
Protestantism. Supposing the president and professors
were Roman Catholics, and they might very well be so,
would they send their children to those classes? They
would not; and they dared not.””  The Rev. Mr.
Armour’s references to Trinity College were rather
breezy, and his proposed epitaph on Tony Traill, the
old bigot of the Parochial, was distinctly clever. He
said :—“ He admitted that Trinity had produced some
of the finest men of the century—men like Lecky and
Lord Justice Fitzgibbon—but these men would have
been great and noble if they had never seen Trinity
College at all. But he said that Trinity College had
always been a kennel of howling dogmatism that had
produced puppies, ecclesiastical and legal, that had been
howling at statesmen who tried to do justice to the
Presbyterian Church or the Roman Catholic Church.
The whole spirit of the institution was summed up in
the present Provost. He was a Christian, but one of
a peculiar type. He could suggest the Provost’s epi-
taph—* Here lies a man who never thought, and never
taught, that there was any education in this planet out-
side the walls of Trinity, especially during his Provost-
ship; or salvation outside the little set of which he re-
garded himself as the patron saint in his life; and who,
even on his deathbed, swore that he was willing to be
hung rather than give up his credo, educational ov
ecclesiastical—one that paid him well in this life, and
that he hoped would be a passport to the isles of the
blessed.’ *’

SOLDIERS IN THE SAWDUST.

OME years ago an English circus company was wont
S to delight the good citizens of Dublin by an annual
ontertainment in the neighbourhood of the Rotunda.
The show was highly popular, and thousands were
nightly thrilled by daring gymnasts and bare-backed
cquestriennes. Many other features common to shows
of the circus variety were provided in the programme,
but the special attraction was usually a life-like repre-

_sentation of the Zulu War, with a sham battle, much

waving of flags and playing of Jingo anthems. The days
of this delectable entertainment are ended, so far as-
Dublin is concerned, but new caterers for the public have
arisen, and the Royal Irish Military Tournament now
supplies the long-felt want. 2

The soldier, in amateur theatricals, has been always
with us. It is quite natural, therefore that he should
also make his appearance in the circus-ring. In fact, his
training suits him far better for the sawdust than the
foot-lights. Qualities of sinew rather than of brain are
requisite for the former, and in these, even in our degene-
rate day, there are soldiers in the British army not defi-
cient. Martial music has a fascination for the multi-
tude, and can be given free scope in a show of this na-
ture, and since the military, moreover, can call upon
great resources in the matter of horse-flesh, all the ingre-
dients for a hippodrome or cirque are at their command.

For all that, the visitor to Ballsbridge during the past
week must have found the entertainment wanting in
some particulars. The indistinct and stiff militaire, who
acted as Master of the Ring, was but a poor substitute
for the dignified, yet affable, gentleman who cracks his
whip and cuts his joke in the civilian circus. The lovely
ladies who elsewhere smile so divinely, and kiss hands
with such grace while imperilling their necks, are sorely
missed. Again, much zest might be imparted to the
monotonous rides on horseback did the daring equestrians
leap through hoops held by chaffing clowns. And what
is any circus, after all, without a clown? Surely out of
the numerous regiments engaged in the exercises of the
Tournament one or two wags could have been obtained
to enliven the proceedings by the old; familiar jests.
These are sad omissions which might easily be supplied.
Perhaps as women have not yet been allowed to enlist we
must forego their presence in the ring, but give us, at
least, the whip, the hoops, and, above all; the clowns.

Let is: not' be supposed, however, that no attempt is
made to live up to the style and title of the entertain-
ment. Now and again the true spirit of the Tourna-
ment of by-gone days animates the proceedings, The
stately jousts between - the knights 'of old are brought
vividly to our recollection as we watch horsemen vieing
with sword and lance, or the men-at-arms striving with
thebayonet. Yet it must: be confessed that soldiers be-
labouring one another’s heads with sticks in the effort to
dislodge paper plumes but faintly suggests the contests
of Ivqnhqe 'a.nd Front de Beeuf, while a tug-of-war, how-
ever inspiring a theme for: the poet’s pen when Greek
joins Greek, is decidedly prosaic when waged with
brewers’ draymen. -

'I.the pomp and circumstance of the tournament find
their fullest expression in the musical Ride and the Tat-
too. It is in things like these that the British beat the
Boe{‘s. The poor Dutch farmers: could shoot fairly
straight, and had some rudimentary knowledge of tac-
tics, but they were hopelessly ignorant of the picturesque
side of military affaivs. One wonders at Spion Kop and

THE UNIVERSITY QUESTION. 
•rT HE University Question has been a long time be-
fore the country. 
During the past quarter of 
,a century there have been moments when all but the 
•fow who never lose their heads have suffered themselves 
to be misled into believing that the question -was on the 
. eve of settlement. Even at such momen~s there have 
• been those who did not forget the lessons of history, 
·who were careful to remember that the British Parlia-
ment· has never granted Ireland any ameliorative mea-
sure from a sense of justice, that all such measures have 
·been conceded solely through fear, or to agitation so 
,•iolent as to amount to social warfare. 
It is high time that all who desi~e the settlement. of 
-the· University Question should seriously grapple with 
• the facts of the situation. 
We have had statements, 
speeches resolutions memorials, ad nauseam. 
They 
have ndt brought the question nearer a settlement. 
Indeed, the prospect' qf a settlement seems more remote 
at this p1·esent moment than it has been for _a genera-
tion. 
The facts must be faced, and one very important 
fact is that mere talk will not bring the problem one 
• iota nearer to a solution. 
The question has been moving about in a fog quite 
!long· enough. 
Snobbery, toadyism, imported respecta-
bility, ,servile imitation of the predominant partner, 
make-believe, 
iHc_onsistency, toying with principles, 
fatuous pusillanimity masquerading as toleration, ·have 
darkenecl the understanding ~nd weakened the will of 
Anglo-Ireland. It is unable to clearly see facts or ~he 
conclusions which they obviously suggest; or seemg 
them, through weakness or ·servility, it shirks the re-
sponsibility of taking action upon them. 
There are 
signs, however, that the era of resolutions, manifestoes, 
speeches, humbug, sunbm·stery and moral cowardice is 
nearing its encl, and that before long, ~ationalist Ire-
land, realising itself, will "scorn to talk and talk, and 
never a deed to do." 
It is unquestionably high time to look at this ques• 
tinn in the clear light of clay. 
We should at once give 
up speeching, passing resolutions, making and renewing 
protests. 
Nationalist Ireland should "go into Com-
mittee of the whole House," and proceed to do -some-
t-hiog practical. 
This conclusion has been forced upon 
the writer by recent happenings, and, most of all, by 
the abortive discussion on the subject to which he lis-
tened at the recent meeting of the Maynooth Union. 
That discussion decided nothing, and led nowhere. 
The " Parliament of the Irish Clergy,'' as one of the 
11peakers called it, assembled, and had before it for dis-
cussion a subject of the highest importance to the mate-
rial, intellectual, national, and religious well-being of 
the country; and the discussion ended in smoke. There 
was only one practical proposal advanced : all else was 
talk, talk, talk. 
Dr. MacDonald, in a speech of extra-
ordinary power, urged that Nationalist Ireland should 
proceed to settle the University Question by going into 
Trinity en masse, and attacking the party of Ascend-
ancy in this citadel. For the proposal which he ad-
vanced, the distinguished Prefect of the Dunboyne Es-
tablishment made a case as strong as it was possible to 
make for such a proposal. 
As far as the writer's ob-
servation went, the proposal, so brilliantly advocated, 
met with little or no favour, and very properly. 
It 
is one which 
Catholic Ireland ought to reject with 
scorn; and what is true of Oatholic Ireland, applies with 
still greater force, if possible, to Irish Ireland. It were 
a sorry sight to see the flower of Catholic and Irish Ire-
land trooping into Elizabeth's souperizing !!,nd dena-
tionalising College, to reside there on . terms of amity 
with the T'raills and the Mahaffys and the Mansion 
House wreckers, and to breathe the malarial atmos-
phere of the place. 
Dr. MacDonald's proposal hacl, however, one supreme 
merit. 
Amidst a wilderness of idle talk and aimless 
discussion, it recommended something definite, tangible, 
real. It set forth a practical scheme. It forces those 
who cannot accept it, who consider themselves bound in 
the interests of Catholicity and of Ireland to oppose it 
to their last breath, to cast about them for a policy 
equally tangible, equally definite, equally practical. 
They must give up talk, and suggest a scheme. 
As far as the writer can see, there are but • three 
schemes between which to choose : -(1) Do as Dr. Mac-
Donald recommends; (2) to proceed at once to force the 
Government to settle the question; or (3) to go about 
settling it ourselves without delay . 
Di:. MacDonald's scheme will not d'o. 
The w1·iter 
cannot accept it, and he hopes the nation will, as in the 
past, continue to reject it. 
One cannot help admiring 
Dr. MacDonald's courage. 
He is a man of rare ability 
of vast attainments, of absolute and transparent 
honesty, but he is, above all, fearless. It is with re-
gret that one finds himself utterly and hopelessly op-
po·sed to such a man on any important question. But 
one cannot help feeling in the present instance that Dr. 
MacDonald has not considered, or has not duly weighed 
all the facts. 
He is prone to accept people at thei;. 
face value; to give 'them credit for honesty equal to 
his own, to regard Jium~n nature a~ being what it ought 
to be rather than what 1t. a?tually 1s. 
He has certainly 
a much more exalted op1111011 of human nature, Irish 
human nature included, than the writer has. 
He does 
not see how any Irish Catholic could possibly desire to 
exchange the Catholic faith for such an abortion as 
Irish Episcopaliai~ Protestantism; neither do I. 
He

l«does not think it lik.ely that Irish Catholics, if only

they. enter the place in sufficiently large numbers, are

. likely to be affected by the realism and infidelity of
Trinity College. I wish I could share his view. He
’does not thi.nk that the nationality of Irish youths
would suffer in Trinity College, if only they enter it en
masse. T wish T could be equally sanguine.

. Reason and experience are against Dr.'MacDonald’s
s view. A tainted atmosphere will work mischief in the
moral just as certainly as in the physical order. The
more unconscious you are that you are breathing poi-
soned air, the more certainly will it do its work; for

the fewer the precautions you will take against it. I

do not anticipate that Catholics going into Trinity

_would suffer in regard to their religious faith or their

national beliefs from any dircct attacks made upon

them. Probably no such attacks would be made; and
if made, they would be resented. What I should fear
is the silent unconscious operation of environment.

Let us see what Dr. MacDonald would have our
| young men do, and for what end. He would have
them go into Trinity College, an unorganised mob,
without leaders, to fight for supremacy against a force
thoroughly organised and officered, in the hope that
possibly they might have a representative on the go-
verning body in about half a century, and that a cen-
tury hence they might have in the Dublin University
the dominant influence which, in a National Univer-
sity, is the right of the majority of the population.

‘What chance is there that this hope will be realised
even in the distant future? Absolutely none. At pre-
sent the occasional Catholic of first-rate ability who
enters Trinity College is allowed—somectimes, at all
events—to reap the reward of his talents, efforts, and
recreancy. IHe becomes a Junior Fellow, but before he
becomes a member of the Board, he is tempted by an in-
viting offer to fare forth, seek other ficlds, and leave
the Ascendancy an absolute monopoly of the power of
the Board.

Suppose that the Catholics enter in a body, will they
receive even-handed justice? I must be pardoned for
doubting it. Renegades to faith or nationality or
both, will, no doubt, receive it in many cases; a few
will receive it in a sufficient number of cases to save
appearances. But what I am absolutely certain of is,
that the moment the Catholic invasion threatens the
domination of the Ascendancy party, that moment the
claims of justice will get short shrift. When, in any
part of this island where the Ascendancy party is do-
minant, I am shown a single example, from out the
past or present, of a tolerant action performed from any
higher motive than to save appearances, or to consoli-
date more thoroughly the power of ascendancy, I shall
believe otherwise; not till then. In Trinity Fellows
are elected after examination. Is there even in Trinity
College itself implicit confidence that justice is always
done.in the election of Fellows? Let the Ascendancy
party itself reply, and the files, not of the Nationalist
- journals, but, of the Tory and Orange sheets.  If any-

. one cares to go back so far he will there find an angry
correspondence about one such clection, where one cor-
respondent, towards the close of the discussion, stated
that it had been made quite clear to all and sundry that
it was quite useless for anyone to stand for a Fellowship
in Trinity College until all the Gwynnes were provided
for. I do not undertake to say whether this statement,
when made, was true or not. What T do say is that
even within the walls of old Trinity there is not abso-
Tute confidence in those who elect to Fellowships. Thoso
outside may, therefore, be excused for rc}usy@ to be-
Jieve that justice would be done, if doing justico should
s ever seriously threaten the domination 91' the party of
Ascendancy.” To hope to capture ?.‘nmty by compet-
ng with all the odds against you, with all the organiza-
 tion and all the generals on ono side, is the most chimeri-

al of dreams.

What are we asked to risk in pursuit_.of this chjmera,l J
We are asked to send our young men into a poisonous
atmosphere. They would have little to fear, perhaps,
from the active hostility or the conscious influence of
their fellow-students of other ‘persuasions. But there -
is the danger which always arises from the tendency in
mixed bodies to avoid, through real or fancied polite-
ness, the discussion of contentious matbers. Frgm.tbls
danger, the concealment or whittling of one’s principles
or opinions in deference to environments, colleges_a.re
no more immune than dinner parties. But there is a
difference. In those colleges in which principles and
views conflict, the process goes on for years and neces-
sarily tends to become habitual; and the young are
more liable than others to regard the obtrusion of con-
tentious matters as ovidencing a lack of good-breeding. 7

The greatest danger, however, is not from the stu- ]
dents, but from one’s preceptors ; and the more f';lgey are
esteemed for their attainments or personal qualities the:
greater the danger. There might be no danger of a
change of one’s religious or political beliefs, but
there might be very great danger, through the
example of one possessing many qualities worthy of ad-
miration, of the growth of indifference. No one would.
be likely to lose himself in admiration of Dr. Traill’s
culture or of Dr. Mahaffy’s refinement, but there are
in Trinity College men of a different stamp.  The
greatest danger of all, however, would be the thought,
for the most part unconscious, ever present to the
Catholic and Nationalist youth That those who would
have the making or marring of his carcer, from whom 3
he would have everything to hope, would be bitterly [
hostile to all his beliefs, and that consequently the
surest way to make his pathway smooth would probably
be to abjure them, or to hide them away out of sight.

I should dread the silent and unconscious sapping of
the nationality of our youths, even more than the effect:
upon their religious belief and practice, and not from
any direct attack, but from the silent and insidious
working of a poisoned atmosphere.

The most important point of all, Dr. MacDonald
curiously overlooks altogether. From the point of view
whether of faith or nationality, no training is just as
pernicious as wrong training, and is far more dangerous.
Surely young men require guidance in these matters.
No University which does not from the outset provide
for the teaching of religion and of national culture to
Irish youths during their most formative years, can be
regarded by the writer as satisfactory, or as otherwise
than an educational and national nuisance. I believe
that this is the view of the great body of Irish Na-
tionalists and Catholics; I certainly hope it is. That it
is the view of Irish Ireland I have no doubt whatever.
Most fervently do I hope, therefore, that Dr. Mac-
Donald’s scheme will continue to be universally re-
garded as quite impossible. -

HE agitation for higher education for Catholics

is rapidly entering upon a new phase. There

are now—since the Maynooth Union meeting—three

policies before the country, each having vigorous pa-

trons—viz., (1) Continue Parliamentary agitation for a

University endowed by Government; (2) Adopt some

scheme of self-help like Father Finlay’s; (3) “Rush

Trinity,”” 4.e., let all our Catholic students at once

flock into Trinity College. So far, the country appears

agreed upon one thing only—the necessity of doing

something. The question is urgent and vital. —The

Trish race, with its faith and civilisation, is going, and
the Jew and the Gentile are taking its place.

At the Maynooth Union last week a Wexford priest
told how the brains and influence of his county are to
meet soon, not merely to concentrate attention upon
this question, thereby arousing interest and understand-
ing, but to take steps towards the selection of one of the
above policies. He suggested that every county in Tre-
land should thus voice its opinion, and that then a
National Council should be summoned by the County
Councils’ General Council to finally voice the wish of

Treland: Never before did the laity so eagerly await .

the result of the bishops’ deliberations in Maynooth.
Never before did the bishops look so anxiously to the
laity for a full and frank expression of their opinion.
Parliamentary agitation has been carried on fruit-
lessly for years and years. Even those who advocate its
continuance say the task is gloomy and practically
hopeless. Not merely the Prime Minister in Parlia-
ment, but Dr. O’Donnell and other expermnccld men ab
the Maynooth Union emphasised that fact. So that if
we want an immediate solution of this long-standing

Father Finlay’s scheme of self-help has aroused great
interest and no little determination in the country.
But the more men have oxamined, it, the more they have

fallen away from it—the more difficulty they see in that

policy. His scheme, at best, is a mere 'chain of proba-
bilities with many a weak link. Cardinal Logue and
Dr. O’Donnell think we could not raise the required
thousands per year. No doubt a shilling a family is
a small tax, buf how many are too poor to do even thaf,
and how many fathers will see their wives and children
starve before, for their sakes, earning that sixpence or
denying themselves its value in porter? Further, what
reason have we to hope for Government endowment
for Father Finlay’s scheme, and finally isn’t the place
in Stephen’s Green too small and cramped even to serve’
as the basis of a National University?

Dr. MacDonald will be found to have correctly
voiced the present sentiment of a majority of the people
of Ireland. In urging the attack upon the citadel of
Trinity College he has put into words the aspirations of
thousands upon thousands of Irishmen, and finally led
them across the Rubicon. There is no use labouring the
point, but Dr. MacDonald will be found to have truly
prophesied the future conduct of that young united
phalanx of the Gael, who, under the blessing of God
and their bishops, will unitedly enter that citadel of the
Saxon, and make Irish and national now what a Pro-
testant Senate and Protestant Government could never
make it.  There is not the slightest danger of our
young men turning traitors there. The bishops know
that a new situation has arrived—not merely in the
break-down of the legislative hope that a false and
faithless Government dangled before their eyes for fifty
years or more, but in the larger number of socially
evolved Catholics who are anxious for a hall-marked Uni-
versity education, in the larger number of prospective
students yearly turned out by the Intermediate Board,
in the new spirit of self-help and advance and self-re-
spect fostered by the Leaper and the Gaelic League.
Wisely and well did our bishops direct the movement;
cautiously moving and waiting until the new spirit
would have grown and ripened into the full certainty
of carrying Trinity without the loss of a man. Our
young fellows have the brains and character to do it.
Fancy the scholarships won—the professorships taken
till in a few years the Senate of Trinity College would
be Irish. A great practical difficulty in the way of
this policy, and one to be overcome before it could be
adopted, is the financial difficulty. Trinity is far too
dear as it stands.

No doubt, the rushing of Trinity would not finally
solve or settle the Irish University Question. But, as
a mere tactical move, it would obtain for us redress in a
quicker time than any other way. I wonder dobs
every Irishman realise all Mr. Balfour felt and meant
the other day when he said he’d “look with dismay upon
Catholics rushing Trinity.”” It would set up so many
complications that almost at once the Irish nation
would obtain an up-to-date University.

All Treland will set eyes on the 28th inst. to see will
Wexford select a policy. Before now Wexford—slow,
but sure—has led the van, and it would have been well
had all Irveland followed. Wexford may be relied on
to dispassionately select the policy that, on its judg-
ment, will secure for the country the highest educa-

A REVIEW OF EDUCATION IN IRELAND.—IV.

The means o'f measuring the value of the teaching and
the Dogrees of Trinity Collego by any recognised academic
standard are very scant; for by pleading its vener-
able age and plmutwgly pointing to the hoary walls that
guard it, those who direct the destiny of that Institution
have always succeeded in shielding it both from public
inspection and from academic comparison with other
teaching Institutions in Ireland. In that it has been
more fortunate than the Queen’s Colleges. The General
Medical Council is, so far as I am aware, the only outside
authority which has any power of inqui and T take
the following from its Report of 1886 *:—“ We feel
bound to state that our joint opinion is, that the Exami-
nation for the Degree of Bachelor of Medicine in the
University of Dublin is inadequate for a degree admit-
ting to the Medical Register; and that the candidates
were not sufficiently tested in Medicine, Surgery, and
Midwifery, inasmuch as there was no viva voce or prac-
tical examination in Pathology, Surgery, Obstetrics, or
Medical Jurisprudence.”’ They add that, with the ex-
ception of the specially medical portion and that in Psy-
siological Anatomy, “in all the other departments the
Examination was unworthy of a University Degree, and
insufficient for a Registrable Qualification.””

“Those who succeed in passing the Examination for
the M.B. of Dublin University are not only admitted to
the Medical Reguster, and have thus conferred on them
all the rights and privileges of registered medical practi-
tioners, but they may also become the happy possessors
of a University Degree, which is usually regarded as a
higher qualification than a mere licence admitting to the
Register. The University of Dublin itself does not, how-
ever, take this view. The University requires for its
mere licence that the candidate should pass through the
same medical curriculum, and undergo the same Exami-

students with a B.A. Degree only eligible for Degrees in
Medicine meant that one should have obtained his B.A.
before going through the curriculum for the Degree in
Faculty. But it appears from the Report that the two
courses may run concurrently, and we can thus measure
the educational value of the B.A. Degree of Trinity Col-
lege. What follows in the Report gives us a more con-
crete measure of it:—The system of permitting the
education for the B.A. to be continued simultaneously
with that for M.B. is most un
injurious to both. So far as could be ascertained, it is
accompanied by no compensatory advantage whatever.
Any Bachelor of Arts might surely be expected to under-
stand the spelling and grammatical construction of a
simple Latin prescription, while a very limited know-
ledge of Botany and Chemistry ought to be sufficient to
prevent any glaring mistakes in the nomenclature of sub-
stances in ordinary use. And for a B.A. to write Uncius
sextos, Drachmas quattuor, Tussu urgente, and other
similar absurdities, is surely intolerable. 5
However desirable it may be for a University Graduate
in Medicine to be also a Graduate in Arts, it is surely
not, worth while running the risk of starving the medical
education for the sake of such a limited knowledge of the
Literee Humaniores as is shewn in the examples given.’’

With such a Report before him, the following observa-
tions of Dr. Laffan cannot be thought too strong:—“ It
passes comprehension, that Graduates of this University
are so scandalously ignorant of the very rudiments of
Latin as is disclosed by the Visitors’ Report. So long
as the M.B. was palmed off on the public for something
that it is not, it seemed anomalous to admit the mere
Licentiates of this University to the M.B. Degree; but
now that the cat is out of the bag the seeming anomaly
disappears. . The writer of this paper has more
than once anticipated the Visitors as to the value of
Trinity College Degrees. When he considered the num-
ber of stupid and idle youths that one meets with, he
was often astonished how it was possible that these crea-
tures could master both the Arts and the Medical Curri-
cula simultaneously. Either would give smart and in-
dustrious youths enough to do. The suspicion crossed
my mind that the whole thing must be a sham and a
fraud, and something very like a sham and a fraud these
